lessons.title: Basics of Inheritance
lessons.summary: Extending existing classes to create new ones

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basic syntax of **inheritance** (the extends keyword)
- The relationship between parent [[class]]es (superclasses) and child classes (subclasses)
- Designing for code reusability

Understanding [[inheritance]] enables you to design large-scale programs efficiently.

contents.title: Inheritance with extends
contents.body: A child class **inherits** from a parent class using the extends keyword.
contents.note: Java's **inheritance** is single inheritance. A class can only have one parent class. Multiple inheritance (extends A, B) is not allowed.

contents.title: The protected Access Modifier
contents.body:
A comparison of [[access modifier]]s closely related to **inheritance**:

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass | Other Packages |
|--------|---|---|---|---|
| private | ○ | × | × | × |
| (default) | ○ | ○ | × | × |
| protected | ○ | ○ | ○ | × |
| public | ○ | ○ | ○ | ○ |

protected means "accessible from subclasses."
Private fields are inherited by subclasses but cannot be accessed directly.
They are accessed through public/protected methods like getters and setters.
contents.note: Package-private (no modifier) is only accessible within the same package and cannot be accessed from subclasses in a different package.

contents.title: The "is-a" Relationship in Inheritance
contents.body:
**Inheritance** should be used when the "A is a B" relationship holds.

✅ Appropriate inheritance:
- Dog is an Animal
- Student is a Person

❌ Inappropriate inheritance:
- Car is an Engine? → This is a "has-a" relationship
 → The Car class should have an engine field (composition)

Use inheritance only for **is-a relationships**, and use composition (having a field) for has-a relationships.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Inheritance**: Use extends to inherit the functionality of a parent class
- Java supports only single inheritance (one extends)
- protected: Accessible from the same package + subclasses
- Private fields are inherited but not directly accessible
- Use inheritance when the **is-a relationship** holds

quizzes.question:
Fill in the blank so that class B inherits from class A.
class B _____ A { }
quizzes.explanation: In Java, the extends keyword is used to inherit a class. Writing class B extends A makes B a subclass (child class) of A, inheriting A's public/protected members.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is the correct description of the protected access modifier?
quizzes.explanation: protected allows access from "classes within the same package" and "subclasses even in different packages." private is accessible only within the same class, and public is accessible from anywhere.

choices.text: Accessible only within the same class
Accessible from the same package and subclasses

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Since B inherits from A, it can call A's hello() method. b.hello() outputs "A" and b.world() outputs "B".

lessons.title: Constructor Chaining
lessons.summary: Calling the parent constructor using super()

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How a child [[class]]'s [[constructor]] calls the parent class's constructor
- Explicit invocation using super()
- The conditions under which an implicit super() is inserted

Without understanding constructor chaining, you won't be able to identify the cause of [[compile]] errors.

contents.title: Basics of super()
contents.body: Use super() in a child class constructor to call the parent class's constructor.
contents.note: super() must be the first line of the constructor. It cannot be used together with this().

contents.title: super() Must Be the First Line of the Constructor
contents.body:
Important rules when calling super() within a constructor:

- super() must be written as the first line of the constructor
- this() (call to another constructor in the same class) and super() cannot be used at the same time
- If the parent has no no-argument constructor, super(arguments) is required

Even if you don't explicitly write super(), the compiler automatically inserts "super()" (no-argument version only).
contents.note: Exam questions frequently test "constructor combinations that cause compile errors."

contents.title: Implicit super() Invocation
contents.body:
Important rules:

1. If super() is not written in the child class constructor, the compiler automatically inserts super() (no-argument version)

2. If the parent class has no no-argument constructor, the child class must explicitly write super(arguments) → Otherwise, a compile error occurs

3. Both super() and this() must be the first line of the constructor, so they cannot be used at the same time

4. Constructor execution order is "parent → child"
   - Executed starting from the topmost Object class

5. If no constructor is defined in a class, a no-argument default constructor is auto-generated. However, once you define any constructor, the default is not auto-generated
contents.note: Exam alert: If the parent has no default constructor and the child doesn't explicitly call super(), a compile error occurs.

contents.title: Patterns That Cause Compile Errors
contents.body: Let's review common mistakes.
contents.note: The "//" in code denotes a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- super(): Calls the parent class constructor
- super() must be the first line of the constructor
- If omitted, super() (no-argument) is auto-inserted
- If the parent has no no-argument constructor, explicit super() is required
- Constructor execution order: parent → child
- super() and this() cannot be used together

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Constructors execute from parent to child. C() → B() → A() are called in order, but since super() executes first, the output order is A → B → C. C's constructor → implicit super() calls B's constructor → implicit super() calls A's constructor → "A " → "B " → "C ".

quizzes.question: Where does a compile error occur in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Since super() is not written in Child's constructor, the compiler implicitly inserts super() (no-argument). However, Parent does not have a no-argument constructor, so a compile error occurs at line 7 (Child()). You need to explicitly write super(value).

quizzes.question: Which statement about super() and this() is correct?
quizzes.explanation: super() calls the parent class constructor, and this() calls another constructor in the same class. Since both must be the first line of the constructor, they cannot be used at the same time.

choices.text: super() and this() can be used together in a constructor
super() can be written anywhere in a method
super() and this() cannot be used together (first-line constraint)
this() calls the parent class constructor

lessons.title: Method Overriding
lessons.summary: Redefining a parent's method in a child class

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- **Override**: Redefining a parent [[method]] in a child [[class]]
- The importance of the **@Override** [[annotation]]
- The clear difference between [[overloading]] and [[overriding]]

Although their names are similar, it is important to distinguish these entirely different concepts.

contents.title: Implementing an Override
contents.body: Redefine a parent method using the **@Override** annotation.
contents.note: **@Override** is optional but strongly recommended. It helps detect typos as [[compile]] errors.

contents.title: Difference Between Override and Overload
contents.body:
Despite similar names, these are entirely different concepts.

| Item | Override | Overload |
|---|---|---|
| English | Override (redefine) | Overload (multiple definitions) |
| Location | Between parent and child class | Within the same class |
| Method name | Same | Same |
| Parameters | Same | Different |
| Return type | Same (covariant return also allowed) | Can differ |
| Annotation | @Override recommended | None |
contents.note: If you add **@Override** and the method is not actually an **override** (e.g., due to a typo), the compiler will report an error.

contents.title: Rules of Overriding
contents.body:
**Overriding** has strict rules:

1. The method name and parameter list must match exactly
2. The return type must be the same or a subtype ([[covariant return]] type)
   - Parent: Object getX() → Child: [[String]] getX() ○
3. The [[access modifier]] must be the same or less restrictive
   - Parent: protected → Child: public ○
   - Parent: public → Child: protected × Compile error
4. The throws clause [[exception]] must be the same or a subclass
5. final methods cannot be overridden
6. [[Static method]]s are not overridden but "hidden"

Override vs Overload:
| Comparison | Override | Overload |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Between parent-child classes | Within the same class |
| Method name | Same | Same |
| Parameters | Same | Different |
| Return type | Same/subtype | Flexible |
contents.note: final methods and final classes indicate a design intent of "no further changes." This is also why the String class is declared final.

contents.title: Using super.methodName()
contents.body: How to call a parent's method from a child class.
contents.note: The "//" in code denotes a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Override**: Redefine a parent method in a child class
- Always add the **@Override** annotation
- Name and parameters must be the same; return type must be the same or a subtype
- Access must be the same or less restrictive
- final methods cannot be overridden
- Use super.methodName() to call the parent's method

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: B.greet() is called. First, super.greet() executes A's greet(), outputting "Hello ". Then System.out.print("World") outputs "World".

quizzes.question: Where does a compile error occur in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: When overriding, the access modifier cannot be more restrictive than the parent's. Changing from public (parent) to private (child) violates this rule and causes a compile error. Access must be the same or less restrictive (since public is the least restrictive, only public is allowed here).

choices.text: private void show() (access is more restrictive)

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between overriding and overloading?
quizzes.explanation: Overriding redefines a method with the same name and parameters across parent-child classes. Overloading defines multiple methods with the same name but different parameters within the same class.

choices.text: Overloading is done between parent and child classes
Overriding redefines a parent method in a child; overloading uses the same name with different parameters
Both only require a different return type

quizzes.question: Complete the code to call the parent class constructor from a child class.
quizzes.explanation: Use extends to inherit a class and super() to call the parent class constructor. implements is used for interface implementation.

lessons.title: Abstract Classes
lessons.summary: Define a common framework and enforce implementation

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The definition and usage of [[abstract class]]es
- How abstract [[method]]s enforce implementation in child classes
- Design patterns using abstract classes

You'll learn the design approach of "providing a common skeleton while leaving the details to each class."

contents.title: Defining and Using Abstract Classes
contents.body: Use the abstract keyword to declare classes and methods.
contents.note: The "//" in code denotes a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Abstract Classes Cannot Be Instantiated
contents.body:
An abstract class is an "incomplete class" and cannot be instantiated using new.

- new Animal() → [[Compile]] error (if Animal is abstract)
- Animal a = new Dog() → OK (if Dog **inherits** Animal and implements all abstract methods)

The purpose of abstract classes is to "provide a common design while leaving the specific implementation to child classes."
contents.note: An abstract class can be defined even without abstract methods (used to prevent instantiation with new).

contents.title: Rules of Abstract Classes
contents.body:
1. An abstract class cannot be instantiated (no new)
2. Abstract method: abstract modifier + no method body + ends with semicolon
   abstract void doSomething();  // No { }
3. A class with abstract methods must be declared as an abstract class
4. A child class must **override** all abstract methods
   → If not, the child class must also be declared abstract
5. Abstract classes can also have regular methods, fields, and [[constructor]]s
6. Abstract methods cannot be private (they must be accessible from child classes)

Use cases for abstract classes:
- Consolidate common logic while enforcing individual implementations
- Template Method pattern (common flow + customization points)
contents.note: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated, but they can define constructors. These are called via super() from child classes.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- abstract class: An incomplete class that cannot be instantiated
- abstract method: No implementation; must be implemented in child classes
- Can also have regular methods, fields, and constructors
- If a child doesn't implement all abstract methods, it must also be abstract
- Used for common logic + enforcing individual implementations

quizzes.question: Which statement about abstract classes is correct?
quizzes.explanation: An abstract class is a class with the abstract modifier and can have regular methods and fields. However, it cannot be instantiated (no new). It is possible to declare a class abstract even without abstract methods.

choices.text: Abstract classes can be instantiated with new
Abstract classes cannot have regular methods or fields
Abstract classes can have regular methods and fields
Abstract methods can be marked private

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The Impl class implements name() to return "Java". The print() method is a regular method of Base that calls name(). Through polymorphism, Impl.name() is invoked, outputting "Name: Java".

quizzes.question: Which line causes a compile error in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The Dog class does not override the abstract method speak(). A class that doesn't implement all abstract methods must be declared abstract. A compile error occurs at line 4 (class Dog extends Animal).

quizzes.question: Arrange the steps of class inheritance and method overriding in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: First define the parent class, then create a child class with extends, and redefine the method using @Override.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Defining [[interface]]s (the implements keyword)
- The differences between [[class]]es and interfaces
- How to implement multiple interfaces at the same time

Interfaces define a contract of "what a class can do."

contents.title: Defining and Implementing Interfaces
contents.body: Define with the interface keyword and implement using implements.
contents.note: Interface [[method]]s are implicitly public abstract. If you don't add public in the implementing class, it causes a [[compile]] error.

contents.title: Choosing Between Abstract Classes and Interfaces
contents.body:
Criteria for deciding which to use:

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Relationship | "is a kind of" | "can do" |
| Multiple | Single inheritance only | Multiple implementations OK |
| Fields | Allowed | Constants only (static final) |
| Constructor | Allowed | Not allowed |
| Example | Animal → Dog | Comparable, Serializable |

Since Java 8, interfaces can have default methods (with implementations).

contents.title: Default Methods and Static Methods
contents.body: Since Java 8, interfaces can also define methods with implementations.
contents.note: Default methods were added in Java 8. They allow adding new methods to existing interfaces without breaking implementing classes.

contents.title: Interface vs Abstract Class
contents.body:
| Feature | Interface | Abstract Class |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple inheritance | ○ (multiple implements) | × (one extends) |
| Constructor | × | ○ |
| Instance variables | × (constants only) | ○ |
| default/static methods | ○ (Java 8+) | ○ |
| Usage | "What it can do" contract | "What it is" common base |

Interface fields:
- Implicitly public static final (constants)
- interface Colors { int RED = 1; } // = public static final int RED = 1;

Guidelines:
- "A dog is a kind of animal" → [[Abstract class]] (is-a)
- "A dog can swim" → Interface (can-do / has-ability)

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- interface: Defines a contract (what a class can do)
- Implemented with implements; multiple interface implementation is possible
- Methods are implicitly public abstract
- [[Default methods]]: Have default implementations (Java 8+)
- Static methods: Called with InterfaceName.method()
- Fields are implicitly public static final (constants only)
- Abstract classes are is-a; interfaces are can-do

quizzes.question:
Fill in the blank with the keyword used when a class implements an interface.
class Dog _____ Runnable { }
quizzes.explanation: The implements keyword is used when a class implements an interface. Unlike extends (inheritance), you can specify multiple interfaces separated by commas.

quizzes.question: Which statement about default methods in Java 8+ interfaces is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Default methods provide a default implementation in the interface. Implementing classes may or may not override them. This allows adding new methods to existing interfaces without breaking existing implementing classes.

choices.text: Must always be overridden in the implementing class
Provides a default implementation; overriding is optional

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The MyCalc class overrides doubleIt to return n * 3 instead. Since c is actually an instance of MyCalc, the overridden method is called, producing 5 * 3 = 15.

quizzes.question: Select all correct conditions for method overriding.
quizzes.explanation: For overriding, the method name and parameter list must be identical. The return type can be the same type or a covariant return type (subclass type). The access modifier must be the same or broader (e.g., expanding protected to public is OK, the reverse is not). Static methods cannot be overridden (they are hidden instead).

choices.text: Method name and parameter list must be identical
Access modifier must be the same or broader
Static methods can also be overridden

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Using the [[instanceof]] [[operator]] to check an object's type
- The difference between **upcasting** and **downcasting**
- Safe [[casting]] to prevent [[ClassCastException]]

This knowledge is essential for leveraging **polymorphism**.

contents.title: Type Checking with instanceof
contents.body: Check whether an object is of a specific type.
contents.note: With [[pattern matching]] for instanceof in Java 16+, you can perform type checking and casting simultaneously.

contents.title: Downcasting Is Dangerous — Always Check with instanceof
contents.body:
**Upcasting** is safe, but **downcasting** risks a ClassCastException.

- Upcasting (child → parent): Always safe. Performed implicitly.
- Downcasting (parent → child): Requires explicit casting. Throws an [[exception]] if the type is wrong.

Always check with **instanceof** before casting.

From Java 16+, "pattern matching instanceof" is available (see Chapter 19).

contents.title: Upcasting and Downcasting
contents.body: Child → parent (**upcasting**) is safe; parent → child (**downcasting**) requires explicit casting.
contents.note: The distinction between [[compile]] errors and runtime errors is important. **Downcasting** compiles successfully, but if the actual type is different, a ClassCastException occurs at runtime.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
| Concept | Description |
|---|---|
| instanceof | Checks the type of an object (null always returns false) |
| Upcasting (child → parent) | Implicit, safe, child-specific methods unavailable |
| Downcasting (parent → child) | Explicit cast required |
- Downcasting to an incompatible type → ClassCastException
- Check with instanceof before downcasting for safety
- Java 16+: Pattern matching for type check + cast simultaneously

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The actual type of obj is B. Since B is a subclass of A, both obj instanceof A and obj instanceof B return true. instanceof checks the actual type hierarchy of the object.

quizzes.question: What happens with the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The actual type of variable a is Animal. The downcast to Dog compiles successfully, but at runtime it cannot convert Animal to Dog, so a ClassCastException occurs.

quizzes.question: What is the result of null instanceof String?
quizzes.explanation: When the left-hand side of instanceof is null, the result is always false. null is not an instance of any type. No NullPointerException is thrown.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Practical usage of **polymorphism**
- The mechanism of calling child [[class]] [[method]]s through parent-type [[variable]]s
- Flexible design enabled by [[polymorphism]]

This tests the combined skills of **inheritance**, **overriding**, and [[interface]]s.

contents.title: A Practical Example of Polymorphism
contents.body: Different child classes can be handled the same way through parent-type variables.
contents.note: Even when adding new shapes (e.g., Triangle), existing loop code doesn't need to change. This is the greatest advantage of **polymorphism**.

contents.title: Three Requirements for Polymorphism
contents.body:
**Polymorphism** requires the following three conditions:

1. An **inheritance** relationship exists (extends / implements)
2. A method is **overridden**
3. A child class object is referenced through a parent class/interface type variable

When all three are met, "the same code exhibits different behaviors" — polymorphism is achieved.

contents.title: Methods of the Object Class
contents.body: Every class implicitly **inherits** from the Object class.
contents.note: If you **override** equals(), you must also [[override]] hashCode(). Two objects that are equal must return the same hashCode (necessary for [[HashMap]] and [[HashSet]] to work correctly).

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Polymorphism**: The same call produces different behavior depending on the type
- Parent type [[array]]s/lists can handle different child classes together
- Adding new types doesn't require modifying existing code
- Every class implicitly **inherits** from Object
- toString(): String representation (useful for debugging/display)
- equals(): Equality check (note: == is reference comparison)
- If you **override** equals() → you must also override hashCode()

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The variable type is A but the actual object is B. Through polymorphism, the method of the actual type (B) is called at runtime, so "B" is output.

quizzes.question: When you override equals(), which method must you also override?
quizzes.explanation: When you override equals(), you must also override hashCode(). This is a Java contract: two objects that are equal must return the same hashCode. This is essential for HashMap and HashSet to work correctly.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: a == b is a reference comparison, and since a and b are different objects, it returns false. a.equals(b) calls the overridden equals(), which checks x==1 && y==2, and since they match, it returns true. The key point is the difference between reference comparison (==) and equality check (equals).
